heading: Step 1: View  database users.
body:
- If it's not already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Edit for the user
  to view their privileges, authentication details, and
  X.509 certificates.

To modify existing users for an Atlas project:

- If it's not already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Edit for the user you
  want to modify.

  You can modify the privileges and authentication details
  assigned to the user. You cannot modify the authentication
  method.

  The following table describes what you can do for each user:

  | User Type                             | Action                                                                                                                                |
  | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | SCRAM authenticated users             | Edit a user's password.                                                                                                               |
  | X.509 certificate authenticated users | Download a new certificate.                                                                                                           |
  | AWS IAM users                         | Modify database access privileges.                                                                                                    |
  | Temporary users                       | Modify the time period the user exists or make the user a permanent user, provided the user's expiration date has not already passed. |

  > **Note:** You cannot change a permanent user into a temporary user.
  > If you change a temporary user into a permanent user, you
  > cannot make it temporary again.

- Click Update User to save the changes.

To delete existing users for an Atlas project using the Atlas UI:

Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.

A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.

Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.

To view the project's Activity Feed:

For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).

Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).

You can create a database user that uses [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you
can't connect to your clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
a new database user using the Atlas UI.

> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).

To view 

Atlas

 database users and X.509 certificates in the

Atlas UI:
